content.heading: Before we start coding — think about this:

blocks.text: You're building a game and you want to greet the player by name. But you don't know who's going to play it. You can't write `let name = 'Alex'` because it won't always be Alex.

items: Could the code figure it out on its own?
Could we tell the program to ask the user for their name?

content.heading: We can make the code interactive!

blocks.text: So far your programs only work with values you wrote yourself. That's not very useful — real programs respond to the person using them. `prompt()` pauses your program, shows a pop-up box, and waits for the user to type something. Whatever they type comes back to you as a string.

blocks.code: let name = prompt('What is your name?')
blocks.desc: Show a pop-up asking 'What is your name?' and store whatever the user types into 'name'

content.heading: What happens if you are prompting for a number?

blocks.text: prompt() always gives you back a string — no exceptions. Even if the user types `42`, you get the text `'42'`, not the number `42`. That means you can't do math with it yet. `'42' + 1` gives you `'421'`, not `43`. The fix: wrap `prompt()` in `Number()` and it converts the answer the moment the user hits OK.

blocks.code: let age = prompt('How old are you?')
blocks.desc: Returns the string '13' even if the user typed 13. Looks like a number, but it's text — math won't work.

blocks.code: let age = Number(prompt('How old are you?'))
blocks.desc: Number() converts the string '13' into the actual number 13. Now you can add, subtract, and multiply normally.

blocks.alt: Visual showing prompt() returning a string vs Number(prompt()) returning a number

blocks.label: Always convert if you're doing math
blocks.text: prompt() returns a string every single time — even if the user types a number. '13' is not the same as 13. If you skip Number(), you'll get weird results like '13' + 1 = '131' instead of 14.

exercises.title: Your first interactive program
tasks: Use prompt() to ask the user for their name.
Store it in a variable called userName.
Use a template literal to log: 'Nice to meet you, [name]! Welcome to Curia.'
Run it and type your name in the pop-up.
exercises.hint: The string inside prompt() is the message the user sees. Whatever they type becomes your variable's value.

exercises.title: Age calculator
tasks: Ask the user how old they are using prompt().
Convert the input to a number with Number().
Calculate how old they'll be in 5 years and in 10 years.
Bonus: log what year they were born (use 2025 as the current year).
exercises.hint: Number(prompt('...')) does both steps at once: asks the question and converts the answer.

exercises.title: The string trap with prompt()
tasks: Run the starter code. When the prompt appears, type: 10
Look at the result. Is it what you expected?
Now look at the second version using Number(). Run that and type 10 again.
Explain the difference in your own words.
exercises.hint: prompt() returns text, not a number — even when the user types digits.

exercises.title: Build a personal fact card
tasks: Use prompt() to ask 3 questions: name, favorite animal, and a lucky number.
Convert the lucky number to a Number.
Build a single string using a template literal that combines all three.
Log the length of their name.
exercises.hint: Ask all three questions first, store all three, then do the math and build the sentence.

challenges.title: The adder adds wrong
challenges.description: This program should add two numbers the user enters. But it always concatenates them instead of adding. Fix it.
challenges.hint: What does prompt() always return? What do you need to do before math?
challenges.explanation: prompt() returns strings. String + String = concatenation. Wrap both in Number(): let num1 = Number(prompt(...)). Now it does actual addition.

challenges.title: The greeting ignores what the user typed
challenges.description: No matter what name the user enters, the greeting says 'Hello, friend!' instead of their name. Why?
challenges.hint: What happens to the value returned by prompt() on line 1?

checks.label: logged a greeting using the user's input

challenges.explanation: The result of prompt() is being thrown away on line 1 — nothing stores it. Line 2 then declares a brand new variable called name with the value 'friend', ignoring what the user typed. Fix: let name = prompt('What is your name?');
